items.title: Fetch only from an explicit domain allowlist
items.description: The scraper may fetch only from a fixed allowlist of authoritative technical documentation domains (for example learn.microsoft.com, docs.python.org, docs.ollama.com, modelcontextprotocol.io), plus an official project's GitHub README or docs only when a human explicitly requests that specific source. Any host not on the list is refused before a request is made.
items.applicability: Every outbound fetch the knowledge-growth scraper attempts.
items.example: A request to enrich knowledge on MCP fetches modelcontextprotocol.io; a request that would resolve to an arbitrary blog or aggregator host is rejected with a clear 'host not allowlisted' message.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: enforce the allowlist on the resolved host after redirects, not only on the initially supplied URL. Test implication: assert a non-allowlisted host and a redirect to a non-allowlisted host are both refused. Generator implication: generated enrichment flows reference allowlisted topics, never free-form URLs. Honest boundary: an allowlist scopes where the scraper goes, not whether a given page is accurate, so source reliability is still tracked per item.

items.title: Rate-limit every source politely
items.description: The scraper applies a conservative per-host request rate and a global concurrency cap, with backoff on errors and on any throttling signal the host returns. It never bursts requests at a documentation site, behaving as a courteous client rather than an aggressive crawler.
items.applicability: All fetching, especially when enriching a topic that spans many pages.
items.example: Enriching a multi-page docs section issues one request every few seconds per host and backs off further on a 429 response.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: make the rate and backoff configurable with a polite default, and honour Retry-After when present. Test implication: assert the scraper spaces requests and increases delay after a throttling response. Generator implication: generated enrichment jobs inherit the global rate policy rather than setting their own. Honest boundary: rate limiting reduces load and the risk of being blocked but does not by itself confer permission to crawl; the robots and terms-of-service rule still governs whether to fetch at all.

items.title: Cache fetched content by content hash
items.description: Fetched content is stored in a local cache keyed by a hash of the normalised content. A repeat fetch that yields the same hash is served from cache and does no further processing, and a conditional request (ETag or Last-Modified) avoids re-downloading unchanged pages.
items.applicability: Repeated enrichment runs over overlapping topics and re-checks of known sources.
items.example: Re-running enrichment on the Python docs hits the content hash, recognises nothing changed, and skips re-summarisation, saving work and avoiding duplicate proposals.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: normalise content (strip volatile markup, timestamps, nonces) before hashing so cosmetic changes do not look like content changes. Test implication: assert an unchanged page is served from cache and a changed page invalidates it. Generator implication: generated flows treat the content hash as the dedup key. Honest boundary: a content hash detects byte-level change, not meaning-level change, so a reworded but equivalent page may still be re-summarised.

items.title: Record citation metadata on every fetch
items.description: Every fetch records, at minimum, the source URL, the page title, the fetch date, and the content hash. This citation metadata travels with any proposal derived from the source so a reviewer can see exactly where a fact came from and when it was retrieved.
items.applicability: All fetched material and any knowledge derived from it.
items.example: A proposal derived from the Ollama docs carries url, title, fetchedUtc, and sourceHash, letting a reviewer open the original and confirm the summary.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: store citation fields as structured columns, not free text, so they can be displayed and audited. Test implication: assert no proposal can be created without complete citation metadata. Generator implication: generated proposals always carry their provenance block. Honest boundary: recorded metadata documents where a fact came from but does not itself verify the fact; reviewer judgement and the confidence field still apply.

items.title: Summarise useful facts; never vendor large raw third-party text
items.description: The scraper extracts and rewrites the useful technical facts in original wording. It must not store or propose large verbatim passages of third-party documentation. What enters the knowledge base is an original summary with a citation back to the source, not a copy of the source.
items.applicability: The transformation step between a fetched page and a knowledge proposal.
items.example: From a docs page on an API option, the scraper proposes a concise original description of the option's behaviour and cites the page, rather than pasting the page's paragraphs.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: cap any quoted snippet to a short, clearly-attributed fragment and forbid bulk paste of page bodies. Test implication: assert proposals stay under a snippet length bound and carry a citation. Generator implication: generated knowledge is original-summary text. Honest boundary: this is a clean-room discipline to respect copyright and keep the base maintainable, not a legal determination of fair use; ambiguous cases default to summarise-and-cite.

items.title: Never scrape secrets or private pages
items.description: The scraper targets only public technical documentation. It must not fetch authenticated, paywalled, or internal pages, must not submit credentials, and must not harvest tokens, keys, or personal data. Pages that require login or appear to expose secrets are skipped, not captured.
items.applicability: Source selection and content handling for every fetch.
items.example: A docs URL that redirects to a login page is abandoned rather than having credentials supplied; a page that incidentally displays a key is not summarised and the key is never persisted.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: detect auth redirects and refuse to send credentials; scrub any secret-shaped strings from captured text. Test implication: assert the scraper skips an auth-gated page and redacts secret patterns. Generator implication: generated enrichment never targets private surfaces. Honest boundary: pattern-based secret detection is imperfect, so the no-private-pages rule is the primary control and redaction is defence in depth.

items.title: Produce proposals, never auto-approved knowledge
items.description: Output of the scraper enters the system strictly as unapproved knowledge proposals. No scraped fact is ever written directly into the approved, prompt-injected knowledge set. The approval lifecycle that governs all knowledge governs scraped proposals identically.
items.applicability: The boundary between web-derived candidates and installed knowledge.
items.example: An enrichment run creates twelve proposals in a pending queue; none influences prompt context until a human approves them.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: route scraped output through the same proposal table and review UI as any other source. Test implication: assert a freshly scraped item has unapproved status and is excluded from prompt-injected knowledge. Generator implication: generated flows never bypass review for web-sourced content. Honest boundary: automation can rank and pre-fill proposals to speed review, but it must not substitute for the human approval decision.

items.title: Detect duplicate sources before proposing
items.description: Before creating a proposal the scraper checks whether the same source (by URL and content hash) or a closely matching existing knowledge item already exists. Duplicates are collapsed into a single proposal or linked to the existing item rather than flooding the review queue with repeats.
items.applicability: Proposal creation across repeated runs and overlapping topics.
items.example: A second run over the same docs page finds the existing proposal by content hash and updates its last-seen date instead of creating a near-identical duplicate.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: dedup on both exact source identity and a similarity check against existing knowledge text. Test implication: assert a re-scrape does not create a second proposal for unchanged content. Generator implication: generated flows surface 'already known' rather than re-proposing. Honest boundary: similarity matching can miss paraphrased duplicates or wrongly merge distinct facts, so borderline cases should still be shown to the reviewer.

items.title: Warn when a source has gone stale
items.description: Each knowledge item derived from the web carries its source fetch date and content hash. A periodic re-check compares the live page against the stored hash; if the source changed, or has not been re-verified within a configured window, the item is flagged stale so a reviewer can refresh or retire it.
items.example: An item sourced a year ago whose underlying docs page now differs is flagged 'source changed, review recommended' on the next re-check.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: store fetch date and hash per item and schedule a bounded re-check that respects the rate limit. Test implication: assert an item past its freshness window or with a changed hash is marked stale. Generator implication: generated knowledge exposes a freshness signal. Honest boundary: a stale flag indicates the source may have moved on, not that the stored fact is necessarily wrong; a human decides whether to update.

items.title: Degrade gracefully when the network is unavailable
items.description: When there is no network, the scraper reports 'web enrichment unavailable' and the application continues using its existing local knowledge. The absence of internet must never block a page, fail a build, or break the core knowledge features; web growth is an enhancement layered on top of a fully offline system.
items.applicability: Any environment without internet, including the platform's default local-first deployments.
items.example: On an air-gapped install, an enrichment request returns a clear unavailable status and the Knowledge page still loads from the local database.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: treat the network probe like other optional-capability probes, fail closed and degrade, never throw on the request path. Test implication: assert enrichment-unavailable does not affect core page loads or tests. Generator implication: generated features check enrichment availability before relying on it. Honest boundary: offline means no new external facts arrive, so the knowledge base only grows from local sources until connectivity and approval resume.

items.title: Treat local document extraction as the offline knowledge path
items.description: The same summarise-and-propose pipeline that processes allowlisted web pages also processes locally supplied documents (HTML, text, imported docs). Offline, knowledge grows by extracting facts from documents the user already holds, using identical provenance, citation, and approval handling with the local file as the source.
items.applicability: Knowledge growth in offline or restricted environments and from imported project documentation.
items.example: With no internet, a user points the extractor at a local HTML manual; it produces the same kind of cited proposals, with the file path and hash as provenance.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: reuse one extraction-and-proposal pipeline for both web and local sources so behaviour and governance are identical. Test implication: assert a local document yields proposals with provenance and unapproved status. Generator implication: generated enrichment treats local docs as a first-class source. Honest boundary: local extraction is only as current and accurate as the supplied document, and the user is responsible for having the right to use it.

items.title: Respect robots directives and terms of service
items.description: Before fetching, the scraper honours the host's robots directives and any stated terms that forbid automated access, identifies itself with an honest user agent, and avoids paths the site asks crawlers to leave alone. Where a site disallows automated retrieval, the scraper does not fetch it even if it is otherwise on the allowlist.
items.applicability: Every fetch decision against an allowlisted host.
items.example: An allowlisted docs host whose robots file disallows a particular path is not crawled on that path, and the scraper sends a descriptive user-agent rather than impersonating a browser.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: fetch and cache robots directives per host and apply them alongside the allowlist. Test implication: assert a disallowed path is skipped even on an allowlisted host. Generator implication: generated jobs cannot opt out of robots handling. Honest boundary: robots and terms encode the publisher's request and reduce risk, but compliance is an operational and policy matter; this rule is engineering practice, not legal counsel.

items.title: Attach provenance and confidence to every proposal
items.description: Each proposal carries its provenance (source identity and fetch context) and an explicit confidence reflecting source reliability and extraction certainty. Reviewers and downstream prompt-injection logic can weigh a proposal by how trustworthy and how well-evidenced it is, rather than treating all proposals equally.
items.applicability: Proposal scoring, review prioritisation, and later retrieval weighting.
items.example: A fact taken cleanly from official docs is proposed with high confidence; a fact inferred from an ambiguous passage is proposed with lower confidence and a note explaining why.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: derive confidence from declared source reliability plus extraction signals, and store it as data. Test implication: assert every proposal has both provenance and a confidence value. Generator implication: generated proposals never omit confidence. Honest boundary: confidence is a heuristic estimate, not a guarantee of correctness, and human review remains the authority.

items.title: A human approves before a proposal becomes installed knowledge
items.description: The transition from proposal to installed, prompt-injected knowledge requires an explicit human approval action. Until approved, a proposal is visible for review but excluded from the curated knowledge that is injected first into prompt context. Approval, edit, and rejection are recorded for audit.
items.applicability: The governance gate at the heart of the knowledge lifecycle.
items.example: A reviewer edits a proposal's wording, then approves it; only then does it join the curated knowledge set and start influencing generated output.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: gate prompt-injection on approved status and record who approved what and when. Test implication: assert unapproved proposals never reach prompt context and that approval is audited. Generator implication: generated knowledge cannot self-promote past review. Honest boundary: human approval improves trust but does not make a fact infallible; items remain versioned and can be re-reviewed or retired later.

items.title: Extract clean main content, discarding boilerplate
items.description: Before summarising, the scraper isolates the meaningful documentation content and discards navigation, ads, cookie banners, and unrelated chrome. Clean extraction improves summary quality, makes content hashing stable across cosmetic changes, and avoids proposing noise.
items.applicability: The parsing stage between fetch and summarisation.
items.example: From a docs page wrapped in a large site template, the extractor keeps the article body and drops the sidebar and footer before summarising.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: prefer structural extraction of the main content region and normalise whitespace before hashing. Test implication: assert boilerplate-heavy input yields a summary focused on the article body. Generator implication: generated proposals reflect content, not page chrome. Honest boundary: content extraction is heuristic and can occasionally drop a relevant aside or keep some boilerplate, so the reviewer sees the cited source to catch misses.

items.title: Require explicit human request to add a GitHub source
items.description: Official project README and documentation on GitHub may be used as a source only when a human explicitly names that repository for enrichment. The scraper does not crawl GitHub broadly or follow arbitrary repository links; it fetches the specific official docs a person asked for and applies the same summarise, cite, and propose discipline.
items.applicability: On-demand enrichment from a named official open-source project.
items.example: A user asks to enrich knowledge from a specific project's README; the scraper fetches that README, summarises it with citation, and proposes the result, without wandering into other repositories.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: scope a GitHub fetch to the explicitly requested repository's docs paths, not its entire content or issue tracker. Test implication: assert a GitHub fetch occurs only for an explicitly requested repo and stays within docs/README paths. Generator implication: generated flows surface GitHub enrichment as an explicit, opt-in action. Honest boundary: a README reflects the project authors' claims, which may be promotional or outdated, so such proposals warrant careful review and an appropriate confidence.

items.title: Audit every fetch and proposal decision
items.description: The scraper records an audit trail of what it fetched, what it skipped and why (not allowlisted, robots-disallowed, cached, private), and what proposals it created or merged. This makes knowledge growth explainable and lets an operator review the scraper's behaviour after the fact.
items.applicability: Operational oversight, debugging, and trust in the growth process.
items.example: An audit log shows three pages fetched, one skipped as non-allowlisted, one served from cache, and four proposals created, each linked to its source.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: log decisions as structured, queryable records correlated to the run and to resulting proposals. Test implication: assert each fetch and skip produces an audit record with a reason. Generator implication: generated growth runs are fully traceable. Honest boundary: an audit trail explains what the scraper did, not whether each proposed fact is correct; that remains the reviewer's judgement.
